summary: Joel Spolsky delivers a scathing review of Sprint's LG Fusic phone and Power Vision Network service, which Sprint sent to bloggers hoping for positive coverage. He systematically dismantles every aspect of the product: the cheap plastic build quality, the confusing menu system with gratuitous animations, two separate MP3 players that are both broken in different ways, and overpriced content services offering public domain movies on a tiny screen. The review builds to a broader critique of telecom companies trying to be content gatekeepers instead of embracing the open internet. He concludes with a satirical theory that the phone must be designed for four-year-olds, since nothing else explains the toy-like design and unusable interface.
key_points: The LG Fusic phone has catastrophically bad industrial design, resembling a Fisher Price toy rather than a premium device, with cheap plastic construction and dangling rubber plug-caps
The phone contains two separate MP3 players, neither of which works properly — one requires a network connection even for local files, and the other stops playing when you close the clamshell
Sprint's content services (music at $5/3 songs, movies at $7/month) are overpriced ripoffs offering public domain garbage that nobody would watch even for free

Telecom companies keep repeating the same mistake of trying to be content gatekeepers and toll collectors instead of enabling open internet access, a pattern Spolsky first wrote about with WAP in 2000
No amount of blogger outreach or tipping point marketing strategy can overcome a fundamentally terrible product
money_quotes: There's no better way to make me not want to write about something than to ask me to write about it.
Believe me, it is hard to make black clash with anything, but LG did it.
Thank you Sun Microsystems for bringing programming language advertisements into consumer electronics.

There are two bad MP3 players on this device, neither one of which remembers where you're up to, neither one of which can be used on the subway with the phone folded in my pocket, neither one of which has a fast-forward feature.
This service is literally as much of a scam as those X-Ray glasses they used to advertise in comic books to steal a few bucks from some little kids.

Cell phone companies keep failing to insert themselves as toll collectors because they're so darn clueless about how the Internet works, and about the value of many-to-many networks instead of broadcast networks.

All the 'tipping point' theories in the world won't protect Sprint from the basic truth that the LG Fusic user interface could basically serve as an almost complete textbook for a semester-long course in user interface design, teaching students of usability exactly what NOT to do.
No amount of pampering bloggers and calling them Ambassadors is going to get around the fact that you're sending us plastic junk phones that look like bath toys.
key_insight: No marketing strategy — not even giving free products to influencers — can compensate for a fundamentally broken user experience and a business model built on exploiting customers rather than serving them.
